steps.prompt: Which is the main verb of the first clause?

hints.sum: 'Sum' is a finite verb but belongs to the subordinate clause after 'quia'

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd plural would be 'videtis' — 'Videte' is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive 2nd plural would be 'videatis' — 'Videte' is imperative
hints.future indicative: Future 2nd plural would be 'videbitis' — 'Videte' is imperative

hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the parts of 'vinco' (to conquer)
hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the parts of 'vivo' (to live)
hints.vito, vitare, vitavi, vitatum: These are the parts of 'vito' (to avoid)

steps.reveal: ipse ego sum = it is I myself (1st sing. pres.)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sim' — 'sum' is indicative

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edo' (to eat)
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'eo' (to go)
hints.do, dare, dedi, datum: These are the parts of 'do' (to give)

steps.reveal: manus meas = my hands (acc. pl., direct object)

hints.nominative singular: Nominative singular 'manus' looks the same — but it is the object of 'videte'
hints.nominative plural: Nominative plural 'manus' also looks the same — but context requires accusative
hints.genitive singular: Genitive singular 'manus' also looks the same (4th decl.) — but 'meas' confirms accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'manus' has genitive in -us
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'manus' has genitive in -us
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'manus' has genitive in -us

steps.reveal: pedes meos = my feet (acc. pl., direct object)

hints.nominative plural: 'Pedes' looks the same in nominative plural, but it is the object of 'videte'
hints.genitive singular: Genitive singular would be 'pedis' — 'pedes' is accusative/nominative plural
hints.ablative plural: Ablative plural would be 'pedibus' — 'pedes' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'pedis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'pedis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'pedis' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What is the function of 'ipse' alongside 'ego'?
steps.reveal: ipse ego = I myself (double emphasis on identity)

hints.reflexive pronoun: Reflexive would be 'se/sui' — 'ipse' is intensive, not reflexive
hints.relative pronoun: Relative would be 'qui/quae/quod' — 'ipse' is an intensive pronoun
hints.demonstrative pronoun (that one): 'Ipse' can mean 'that very one' but with 'ego' it means 'I myself'

steps.prompt: What type of clause does 'quia' introduce?
steps.reveal: quia ipse ego sum = because it is I myself / that it is I myself

hints.relative clause: Relative clauses use 'qui/quae/quod' — 'quia' is a causal conjunction
hints.purpose clause: Purpose clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — 'quia' introduces a causal/declarative clause
hints.result clause: Result clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — 'quia' here means 'because/that'

translation: See my hands and my feet, that it is I myself
